text:
Title: Rhein exerts anti-diabetic nephropathy effects by suppressing the Rac1/NOX1/β-catenin signaling pathway Background: Diabetic nephropathy (DN) is one of the main microvascular complications of diabetes mellitus. Searching for effective treatment for DN is a topic of considerable research interest. Rhein has been reported to possess potential therapeutic effects for diabetic nephropathy. However, the molecular mechanism is still unclear. In this study, we explored rhein's anti-diabetic nephropathy effects and its impact on the Rac1/NOX1/β-catenin signaling pathway.

To verify the impact of rhein on HG-induced podocyte injury and STZ-induced DN, the cell viability was evaluated by CCK-8 assay and the ROS production by flow cytometry. In addition, the relative expression levels of Rac1, NOX1 and β-catenin were measured by RT-qPCR. The expression of GPX4, α-SMA, Nephrin and Podocin was examined by IF. The renal pathological changes and the extent of tubulointerstitial fibrosis were detected by HE and Masson staining, and the expressions of protein and metabolic related molecules were detected by IHC and corresponding biochemical kits.

Background Rhein has been shown to possess remarkable protective effects against a range of diseases, but its influence on high glucose (HG) induced podocyte injury and the underlying molecular mechanism remains unclear. Methods & Findings Human podocytes were treated with HG in the presence or absence of rhein. Rhein treatment attenuated HG-induced oxidative stress, iron accumulation (Fe2+), and expression of ferroptosis and mesenchymal markers (TFR1, ACSL4, α-SMA, Vimentin, Snail, Desmin). Rhein was then found to inhibit the Rac1/NOX1/β-catenin signaling pathway. Through constructing Rac1 siRNA vector, it was confirmed that the effects of rhein on MDA, Fe2+, and target gene expression were mimicked by silencing Rac1. Furthermore, by transfecting Rac1 over-expression vector, the effects of rhein on reducing SOD, GSH and GPX4, as well as down-regulating SLC7A11 and Nephrin, were confirmed. In vivo, lentiviral-mediated Rac1 overexpression abolished the beneficial effects of rhein on ferroptosis and fibrosis. Conclusion Rhein mitigates HG-induced ferroptosis of podocytes via inhibition of Rac1/NOX1/β-catenin pathway.

Conclusion: Rhein exerts anti-diabetic nephropathy effects through inhibition of ferroptosis and epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) and through regulation of the Rac1/NOX1/β-catenin signaling pathway.
